nativeRange.habitat: River main channels, floodplain lakes, sandbars, and vegetated banks; tends to prefer high-oxygen environments and is not typically found in acidic blackwater systems.

diet.notes: Snails are a dietary essential for the Amazon Puffer. Like all puffers, this species has fused, beak-like teeth that grow continuously throughout its life and must be worn down by crunching hard-shelled prey — pond snails, bladder snails, and nerite snails are all suitable. Without regular access to snails, the teeth can overgrow to the point of impairing feeding. The diet can be rounded out with frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, chopped mussel, cockle, and earthworms. In the wild, this species also grazes biofilm and algae from surfaces opportunistically.

avoidWith: invertebrates (will eat snails used for tooth maintenance if not dosed deliberately)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by lwolfartist, 4496x3000. Sharp, high-res side profile showing the diagnostic yellow-green dorsum with dark saddle blotches, pale belly and translucent fins; whole fish in frame. Best overall image. Filed generically ('Georgia aquarium') so exact species label carries mild ID caveat, but morphology matches Colomesus asellus.

Photo by O. Schneider (Assel-Kugelfisch = German for Amazon Puffer), 1090x723. Live aquarium individual, whole fish in frame showing olive-yellow body with dark blotched banding; colours a touch muddy but correctly ID'd.

Photo by Neale Monks, 669x479. Different individual among plant stems, clearly showing the clean white belly and green-edged dark bars; lower resolution and dim lighting, kept only as a non-recommended extra.

summary: The Amazon Puffer is widely considered the only schooling puffer species in the hobby, best kept in groups of four or more. Unlike most puffers, it is a true freshwater fish. Regular access to snails is essential for dental health, and tankmates should be robust enough to tolerate occasional fin-nipping.
careNotes:
The Amazon Puffer (Colomesus asellus) is widely considered the only known schooling puffer species — a notable exception in a family (Tetraodontidae) otherwise characterized by solitary or territorial behavior. It should be kept in groups of at least four; a group of 4–6 in 200 liters is a reasonable starting point, with larger tanks preferred as group size increases.

Unlike many puffers sold in the aquarium trade, the Amazon Puffer is a true freshwater species and does not require or benefit from brackish conditions.

Most specimens in the trade are wild-caught, and captive-bred individuals are rarely available. Wild imports have been noted to be prone to white spot (ich) and are generally advised to undergo an extended quarantine period before being added to a display tank. Water quality must be kept excellent, as the species tends to be intolerant of elevated organic waste.

Fin-nipping toward smaller or slower tankmates has been documented; small tetras, rasboras, and long-finned varieties are best avoided. Robust medium-to-large fish make better companions.

Snails provided for tooth maintenance should be offered in a controlled and consistent way to ensure the puffer always has access to hard-shelled prey. Note that the puffer will seek out and consume any snail it can find in the tank, which should be factored in when choosing other inhabitants.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been recorded. In the wild, spawning is reported to occur in main river channels and tributary mouths during high-water periods, with larvae developing in floodplain nursery zones. No practical captive breeding protocol is documented in hobby sources.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12.8 cm SL), native range (Amazon basin Peru to Marajó Island, Orinoco/Essequibo basins), water temperature (22–28°C), pH (5.5–7.2), and hardness (5–15 dH). Temperature range averaged with SeriouslyFish (20–26°C) to produce 21–27°C. Aquarium Co-Op (primary in source-urls.json) returned 404; SeriouslyFish promoted to primary.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented. Wild spawning occurs in main channels during high-water season.
